Records: 500 patients

Duration: 6 Hours

Imputation Methods

Outlier Techniques
Z-Score, IQR, %tile, Wins
Project Overview

Patient health dataset with intentional missing values and extreme measurements. The goal is to produce a clean, ML-ready dataset for predicting disease risk.

📋 Dataset Schema
ColumnTypeMissingStatus
patient_idInt0✓ Complete
ageFloat → Int24 (4.8%)Missing
genderCategorical25 (5.0%)Missing
regionCategorical24 (4.8%)Missing
bmiFloat25 (5.0%)Missing
blood_pressureFloat0✓ Complete
cholesterolFloat25 (5.0%)Missing
glucoseFloat24 (4.8%)Missing
disease_riskBinary (0/1)0✓ Target
🗺 Region Distribution
❓ Missing Values by Column
🎯 Disease Risk Split

MetricAgeBMIBlood PressureCholesterolGlucose

Mean49.7626.57125.17203.41101.33
Std Dev18.254.9517.3243.0320.71
Min18.011.7074.20101.8042.00
25th %tile34.7523.75114.20177.0088.38
Median50.026.30125.40199.70102.30
75th %tile66.029.35136.22227.70114.00
Max79.059.97200.00447.56165.20
Part A: Missing Value Imputation

Six strategies were applied and compared. Each method has different assumptions about data distribution and relationships between features.

Task 2a — Simple Imputer (Mean)
Numerical – BMI
Score: 3/10
Replaces NaN with column mean (26.57). Simple but shifts distribution when outliers exist. Not recommended for skewed medical data.
Task 2a — Simple Imputer (Median)
Numerical – BMI
Score: 4/10
Replaces NaN with median (26.30). More robust than mean — resistant to outliers. Preferred over mean for clinical values.
Task 2b/2c — Most Frequent
Categorical – Gender, Region
Score: 4/10
Fills gender NaNs with "Female" (mode) and region NaNs with "South". Correct approach for categorical data — preserves class balance.
Task 2d — Random Sample
+ Missing Indicator
Score: 6/10
Creates binary flag columns for missingness, then fills NaN with randomly sampled observed values. Preserves original distribution shape better than mean/median.
Task 2e — KNN Imputer
k=5 Neighbors
Score: 8/10
Finds 5 most similar patients and uses their values for imputation. Multivariate — considers BMI, BP, cholesterol together. Smart and context-aware.
Task 2f — MICE Algorithm
10 Iterations
Score: 10/10
Iterative chained equations — models each column as a function of all others across 10 passes. Most statistically sound. Best for correlated medical data.
📈 Method Effectiveness Comparison
🔬 BMI Distribution — Before vs After MICE
📊 Imputation Results Summary
StrategyColumn(s)Missing BeforeMissing AfterData LossVerdict
Mean ImputerBMI250None⚠ Acceptable
Median ImputerBMI250None✓ Good
Most FrequentGender, Region490None✓ Good
Random SampleAge, BMI, Chol, Gluc980None✓ Good
KNN (k=5)All numerical1220None✓✓ Great
MICE (10 iter)All numerical1220None🏆 Best
Part B: Outlier Detection & Treatment

Four outlier handling methods were applied on the MICE-imputed dataset. Methods range from complete removal to gentle capping.

📉 Z-Score Method (Task 3a)

Detects patients with extreme cholesterol or glucose values (|z| > 3). Suitable for normally distributed features.

Cholesterol outliers5 patients
Glucose outliers1 patient
Threshold: |z| > 3  |  Total removed: 6 rows  |  Result: 494 rows
📦 IQR Method (Task 3b)

Interquartile range detects unusual BMI values. More robust than Z-Score — does not assume normality.

BMI outliers (IQR fence)14 patients
Blood Pressure outliers3 patients
BMI: Q1=24.0, Q3=29.1, IQR=5.1  |  Total removed: 17 rows  |  Result: 483 rows
✂️ Percentile Method (Task 3c)

Caps values below 1st percentile and above 99th percentile. Non-destructive — no rows removed, extremes are clipped.

ColumnP1 (lower cap)P99 (upper cap)Values Capped
BMI14.9037.90~10
Blood Pressure89.30162.50~10
Cholesterol127.30315.20~10
Glucose61.00145.60~10
Rows lost: 0  |  Dataset shape: 500 rows preserved
🏆 Winsorization (Task 4) WINNER

Replaces extreme values (5% from each tail) with the 5th and 95th percentile boundary values. Smoothly caps without removing any patient records.

ColumnBefore MaxAfter MaxBefore MinAfter Min
BMI60.0032.8011.7019.20
Blood Pressure200.00150.8074.2098.40
Cholesterol400.00267.80101.80146.90
Glucose165.20135.5050.0065.00
✅ Rows lost: 0  |  All 500 patient records preserved
🧪 Outliers Detected Per Method
📦 Box Plots — Before vs After Winsorization
📊 Outlier Method Comparison
MethodRows RemovedData LossAssumptionBest ForVerdict
Z-Score (|z|>3)61.2%Normal distributionSymmetric data✓ Good
IQR Method173.4%NoneNon-normal, robust✓ Good
Percentile Cap00%NoneAny distribution✓✓ Great
Winsorization00%NoneHealthcare / small data🏆 Best
Part C: Analysis & Key Findings

Data-driven insights extracted from the cleaned dataset. All findings are based on computed statistics from the final 500-patient dataset.

🏆 Best Imputation: MICE Algorithm

The MICE (Multiple Imputation by Chained Equations) algorithm outperformed all other methods by iteratively modelling each feature as a function of all other features over 10 passes.

Why MICE wins:
✓ Models inter-feature correlations (BMI ↔ cholesterol ↔ glucose)
✓ Produces statistically unbiased imputed values
✓ Works for both linear and non-linear relationships
✓ Most appropriate for correlated clinical measurements
🏆 Best Outlier Treatment: Winsorization

Winsorization (5% from each tail) replaced extreme values with boundary percentile values — preserving all 500 patient records while eliminating distortion from extreme measurements.

Why Winsorization wins:
✓ Zero data loss — all 500 rows preserved
✓ Smoothly caps extremes without discarding records
✓ Ideal for healthcare where every record is valuable
✓ Reduces std deviation without changing N
🔗 Feature Correlation with Disease Risk
0.287
BMI
0.268
Glucose
0.247
Blood Pressure
0.234
Cholesterol
0.083
Age
🔬 BMI vs Glucose — Risk Segmentation

High-risk patients (red) cluster in the upper-right zone — higher BMI and higher glucose values. This confirms that elevated BMI (overweight/obese range) combined with poor glucose control is a strong predictor of disease risk.

🎯
BMI is #1 PredictorHighest correlation (0.287) with disease risk. Patients with BMI > 29 are disproportionately high-risk.
🍬
Glucose Ranks #2Fasting glucose (r=0.268) is the second-strongest predictor — aligns with clinical diabetes/pre-diabetes indicators.
💓
Blood Pressure #3Systolic BP correlation (r=0.247) confirms hypertension as a key cardiovascular risk driver.
🧪
Cholesterol #4Moderate correlation (r=0.234). Elevated cholesterol (>240 mg/dL) is more prevalent among high-risk patients.
📍
Regional BiasSouth region has 31% of patients (155/500). Regional health access and lifestyle differences may explain distribution skew.
⚖️
Near-Balanced Target46.2% high-risk vs 53.8% low-risk — excellent class balance for binary classification. No upsampling needed.
🚫
Zero DuplicatesNo duplicate patient records found in the original dataset. Data integrity is intact across all 500 records.
✅
ML-Ready OutputFinal dataset: 500 rows × 11 columns, 0 missing, label-encoded categoricals, clipped numerics. Ready for sklearn pipelines.
